channel.title: Oracle on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Oracle on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] ORA-00600: Internal Error
item.description:
ORA-00600: Internal Error

ORA-00600 is an Oracle internal error that indicates a bug in the Oracle kernel code. It is raised when the database encounters an unexpected internal condition that it cannot handle gracefully.

Common Causes

  • A known Oracle bug triggered by specific data patterns or operations
  • Data corruption in tablespaces or data blocks
  • Insufficient resources (memory, file descriptors) causing internal failure
  • Running an Oracle version with known ORA-00600 defects

How to Fix

Check the Trace File

# Trace files are in the dump directory
ls -lt $ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms/<db_name>/<instance>/trace/
# Look for files with "ora" in the name

Search for the First Argument

# The error format is: ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [arg1], [arg2], ...
# Search Metalink/My Oracle Support for the first argument
# e.g., ORA-00600: [kcbz_check_objd_typ_3]

Apply Latest Patch Set

# Check current version
SELECT * FROM v$version;

# Download and apply latest CPU/PSU from Oracle Support

Check for Block Corruption

-- Run RMAN block check
BACKUP VALIDATE CHECK LOGICAL DATABASE;

-- Check specific tablespace
SELECT * FROM v$database_block_corruption;

Try to Open Database in Restricted Mode

-- If database won't open
STARTUP MOUNT;
ALTER DATABASE OPEN RESTRICTED;

Examples

-- Internal error during query
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id = 123;
-- ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [kdsgrp1], [], [], [], [], [], [], []
-- Fix: check for block corruption with RMAN

-- Error during DDL
ALTER TABLESPACE users ADD DATAFILE;
-- ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [4194], [30], [25], [], []
-- Fix: apply latest patch set

Related Errors

  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old
  • ORA-04031 — shared memory allocation failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-01555: Snapshot Too Old
item.description:
ORA-01555: Snapshot Too Old

ORA-01555 occurs when a long-running query needs to read data that has been overwritten in the undo (rollback) tablespace. The undo data needed for consistent read is no longer available.

Common Causes

  • The undo tablespace is too small for the workload
  • A long-running query references data modified by concurrent transactions
  • The UNDO_RETENTION setting is too low
  • The undo tablespace has autoextend disabled

How to Fix

Increase Undo Retention

ALTER SYSTEM SET undo_retention = 3600; -- 1 hour

Resize the Undo Tablespace

ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/undotbs01.dbf' RESIZE 20G;
-- or add a new datafile
ALTER TABLESPACE undotbs1 ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/undotbs02.dbf'
 SIZE 10G AUTOEXTEND ON;

Enable Guaranteed Undo Retention

ALTER TABLESPACE undotbs1 RETENTION GUARANTEE;

Optimize Long-Running Queries

-- Break long queries into smaller batches
-- Instead of:
SELECT * FROM large_table;

-- Use:
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 100000;
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id BETWEEN 100001 AND 200000;

Check Current Undo Usage

SELECT tablespace_name, SUM(bytes) / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM dba_undo_extents
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Examples

-- Long-running report
SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(4) */ * FROM sales WHERE quarter = 'Q1';
-- ORA-01555: snapshot too old: rollback segment number 5 with name "_SYSSMU5$"
-- Fix: increase undo_retention or optimize the query

-- Concurrent DML during report
-- Session 1: long query
-- Session 2: DELETE FROM sales WHERE ...
-- Fix: use UNDO_RETENTION GUARANTEE

Related Errors

  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure
  • ORA-08177 — serialization access failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-01652: Unable to Extend Temp Segment
item.description:
ORA-01652: Unable to Extend Temp Segment

ORA-01652 occurs when Oracle cannot allocate space for a temporary segment in the temporary tablespace. This typically happens during large sorting, hashing, or index creation operations.

Common Causes

  • The temporary tablespace is too small for the operation
  • A query requires a large sort area that exceeds temp space
  • The temporary tablespace datafile cannot auto-extend
  • A runaway query is consuming all available temp space

How to Fix

Check Temporary Tablespace Usage

SELECT
 tablespace_name,
 SUM(bytes_used) / 1024 / 1024 AS used_mb,
 SUM(bytes_free) / 1024 / 1024 AS free_mb
FROM v$temp_space_header
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Add Datafile to Temporary Tablespace

ALTER TABLESPACE temp ADD TEMPFILE '/u01/oradata/temp02.dbf'
 SIZE 10G AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 1G MAXSIZE 30G;

Resize Existing Tempfile

ALTER DATABASE TEMPFILE '/u01/oradata/temp01.dbf' RESIZE 20G;

Kill Runaway Sessions

SELECT sid, serial#, sql_id, temp_space_allocated / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM v$rsql
WHERE temp_space_allocated > 100 * 1024 * 1024;

ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION 'sid,serial' IMMEDIATE;

Optimize the Query

-- Add indexes to reduce sort operations
CREATE INDEX idx_large ON large_table(sort_column);

-- Use hints to avoid sorting
SELECT /*+ NO_SORT */ * FROM large_table ORDER BY col;

Examples

-- Large sort operation
SELECT * FROM million_row_table ORDER BY created_date;
-- ORA-01652: unable to extend temp segment by 128 in tablespace TEMP
-- Fix: ALTER TABLESPACE temp ADD TEMPFILE ...

-- Index creation
CREATE INDEX idx_big ON huge_table(col);
-- ORA-01652: unable to extend temp segment by 256 in tablespace TEMP
-- Fix: increase temp tablespace size

Related Errors

  • ORA-04031 — shared memory allocation failure
  • ORA-012899 — value too large for column

item.title: [Solution] ORA-04031: Unable to Allocate Shared Memory
item.description:
ORA-04031: Unable to Allocate Shared Memory

ORA-04031 occurs when Oracle cannot allocate a contiguous block of shared memory from the shared pool. The SGA is fragmented or too small for the current workload.

Common Causes

  • The shared pool is too small for the application’s cursor usage
  • Excessive hard parsing due to lack of bind variables
  • Shared pool fragmentation from many small allocations
  • Large PL/SQL packages or cursor caching consuming shared memory

How to Fix

Check Shared Pool Size

SELECT name, value / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM v$sga
WHERE name IN ('Shared Pool', 'Large Pool', 'Java Pool');

Increase Shared Pool Size

ALTER SYSTEM SET shared_pool_size = 4G SCOPE=BOTH;

Flush Shared Pool

-- Temporary fix: flush shared pool
ALTER SYSTEM FLUSH SHARED_POOL;

Use Bind Variables to Reduce Parsing

-- WRONG: hard parse every time
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' || 123;

-- CORRECT: bind variables reduce shared pool usage
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :1' USING 123;

Enable Automatic Memory Management

ALTER SYSTEM SET sga_target = 16G SCOPE=BOTH;
ALTER SYSTEM SET sga_max_size = 16G SCOPE=SPFILE;

Examples

-- Cursor exhaustion
BEGIN
 FOR i IN 1..100000 LOOP
 EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT count(*) FROM tab' || i;
 END LOOP;
END;
-- ORA-04031: unable to allocate 4192 bytes of shared memory

-- Fix: use bind variables and increase shared_pool_size

Related Errors

  • ORA-00600 — internal error
  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-08177: Can't Serialize Access
item.description:
ORA-08177: Can’t Serialize Access

ORA-08177 occurs when a serializable transaction attempts to modify data that has been modified by another committed transaction since the serializable transaction started. This is a concurrency conflict at the SERIALIZABLE isolation level.

Common Causes

  • The transaction is running at SERIALIZABLE isolation level
  • Another session modified the same rows between the SELECT and UPDATE
  • High concurrency on the same table rows
  • Long-running serializable transactions increase conflict window

How to Fix

Use READ COMMITTED Isolation Instead

ALTER SESSION SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;

Implement Optimistic Locking

-- Add a version column
ALTER TABLE orders ADD version NUMBER DEFAULT 1;

-- Check version during update
UPDATE orders
SET status = 'shipped', version = version + 1
WHERE id = 100 AND version = 5;

-- If rows updated = 0, another session modified the row

Reduce Transaction Duration

-- Commit more frequently to reduce conflict window
-- Break large transactions into smaller ones

Retry the Transaction

-- In application code, catch ORA-08177 and retry
BEGIN
 -- transaction
 COMMIT;
EXCEPTION
 WHEN OTHERS THEN
 IF SQLCODE = -8177 THEN
 ROLLBACK;
 -- retry after brief wait
 END IF;
END;

Use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

-- Avoid blocking by skipping locked rows
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;

Examples

-- Serializable transaction conflict
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
SELECT * FROM inventory WHERE product_id = 1; -- sees qty = 10
-- Another session: UPDATE inventory SET qty = 5 WHERE product_id = 1; COMMIT;
UPDATE inventory SET qty = 8 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- ORA-08177: can't serialize access

Related Errors

  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old
  • ORA-00600 — internal error

item.title: [Solution] ORA-12899: Value Too Large for Column
item.description:
ORA-12899: Value Too Large for Column

ORA-12899 occurs when an INSERT or UPDATE tries to store a value that is wider than the column’s defined size. Oracle enforces strict column length limits.

Common Causes

  • The value being inserted exceeds the column’s VARCHAR2/CHAR size
  • Multi-byte character encoding requires more bytes than expected
  • The column was defined with a smaller size than required
  • Implicit conversion from a larger type expands the data

How to Fix

Check Column Definitions

SELECT column_name, data_type, data_length, char_length
FROM user_tab_columns
WHERE table_name = 'USERS';

Widen the Column

ALTER TABLE users MODIFY email VARCHAR2(255);

Truncate the Value Before Inserting

INSERT INTO users (name, email)
VALUES ('Alice', SUBSTR('very_long_email@example.com', 1, 100));

Check Character Set Impact

-- AL32UTF8 uses up to 4 bytes per character
SELECT lengthb('café'), length('café') FROM dual;
-- 5 bytes, 4 characters

Use CLOB for Large Values

ALTER TABLE users ADD notes CLOB;

Examples

-- Column too small
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('very.long.email.address@domain.com');
-- ORA-12899: value too large for column "APP"."USERS"."EMAIL" (actual: 30, maximum: 20)

-- Fix: ALTER TABLE users MODIFY email VARCHAR2(100);

-- Multi-byte character issue
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Übergrößenmaßstab');
-- ORA-12899 in UTF8: actual 38, maximum 20
-- Fix: increase column size to account for multi-byte characters

Related Errors

  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure
  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old

item.title: [Solution] Python ImportError: oracledb not found — ModuleNotFoundError Fix
item.description:
ImportError: oracledb not found — ModuleNotFoundError Fix

An ImportError: oracledb not found or ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'oracledb' means Python cannot locate the oracledb package.

What This Error Means

oracledb is the Oracle Database Python driver. It is the successor to cx_Oracle. It is not part of the standard library.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: oracledb not installed
import oracledb # ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'oracledb'

# Cause 2: Installed for wrong Python version

How to Fix

Fix 1: Install with pip

pip install oracledb

# For a specific version
pip install oracledb==2.0.0

Fix 2: Install in the correct virtual environment

source venv/bin/activate
pip install oracledb
python -c "import oracledb; print(oracledb.__version__)"

Related Errors

  • /languages/python/importerror-sqlalchemy/ — ImportError: sqlalchemy
  • /languages/python/importerror-pymysql/ — ImportError: pymysql
  • /languages/python/importerror-psycopg2/ — ImportError: psycopg2

item.title: Oracle - ORA-00054: resource busy (NOWAIT)
item.description:
ORA-00054: resource busy and acquire with NOWAIT specified or timeout expired occurs when a DDL or SELECT FOR UPDATE statement tries to access a table that is currently locked by another session, and the NOWAIT option prevents waiting.

Common Causes

  • Another session holds an exclusive lock on the table
  • Long-running transaction holding locks
  • DDL operation on a table with active DML
  • SELECT FOR UPDATE with NOWAIT on locked rows
  • Application not releasing connections/locks promptly

How to Fix

  1. Check what is locking the table:
SELECT s.serial#, s.sid, s.username, l.locked_mode, o.object_name
FROM v$locked_object l
JOIN dba_objects o ON l.object_id = o.object_id
JOIN v$session s ON l.session_id = s.sid
WHERE o.object_name = 'EMPLOYEES';
  1. Remove the blocking session:
-- Kill the blocking session
ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION 'sid,serial#' IMMEDIATE;
  1. Use WAIT with timeout instead of NOWAIT:
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100
FOR UPDATE WAIT 30; -- wait up to 30 seconds
  1. Use LOCK TABLE with WAIT option:
LOCK TABLE employees IN EXCLUSIVE MODE WAIT 60;
  1. Check for lock chains:
SELECT
 s.sid, s.serial#, s.username, s.blocking_session,
 o.object_name, l.locked_mode
FROM v$locked_object l
JOIN dba_objects o ON l.object_id = o.object_id
JOIN v$session s ON l.session_id = s.sid;
  1. Reduce transaction duration to minimize lock contention:
-- Commit frequently during batch operations
BEGIN
 FOR rec IN (SELECT * FROM large_table) LOOP
 -- process
 COMMIT; -- release locks periodically
 END LOOP;
END;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-00054: resource busy and acquire with NOWAIT specified
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100 FOR UPDATE NOWAIT;
-- ORA-00054: resource busy and acquire with NOWAIT specified or timeout expired

-- Fix: use WAIT instead
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100 FOR UPDATE WAIT 30;

Related Errors

  • Flashback error
  • Trigger error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-00942: table or view does not exist
item.description:
ORA-00942: table or view does not exist occurs when a SQL statement references a table, view, or synonym that cannot be found. This can be caused by missing schema qualification, typos, or insufficient privileges.

Common Causes

  • Table or view does not exist in the current schema
  • Missing schema prefix (e.g., HR.employees vs employees)
  • Table was dropped or renamed
  • Synonym points to non-existent object
  • Missing SELECT privilege on the table

How to Fix

  1. Verify the table exists:
SELECT table_name FROM user_tables WHERE table_name = 'EMPLOYEES';
-- Or check all schemas
SELECT owner, table_name FROM all_tables WHERE table_name = 'EMPLOYEES';
  1. Use fully qualified table names:
-- Instead of
SELECT * FROM employees;

-- Use
SELECT * FROM hr.employees;
  1. Check for typos in table/view names:
-- Case-sensitive names must be quoted
SELECT * FROM "MyTable";
  1. Check synonyms:
SELECT synonym_name, table_owner, table_name
FROM user_synonyms
WHERE synonym_name = 'EMPLOYEES';
  1. Verify object privileges:
SELECT grantee, table_name, privilege
FROM user_tab_privs
WHERE table_name = 'EMPLOYEES';
  1. Create missing table or synonym:
-- Create synonym
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM employees FOR hr.employees;

-- Or grant access
GRANT SELECT ON hr.employees TO myuser;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-00942: table or view does not exist
SELECT * FROM employees;
-- If table is in HR schema

-- Fix: qualify with schema
SELECT * FROM hr.employees;

-- Error can also indicate insufficient privileges
SELECT * FROM v$session;
-- ORA-00942: table or view does not exist
-- (actually means no SELECT on v$session)

-- Fix: grant access
GRANT SELECT ON v_$session TO myuser;

Related Errors

  • Permission error
  • Partition error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-01017: invalid username/password
item.description:
ORA-01017: invalid username/password; logon denied occurs when Oracle cannot authenticate the user credentials. The database rejects the login attempt due to incorrect username or password.

Common Causes

  • Incorrect username or password
  • Case sensitivity in password
  • Account locked after too many failed attempts
  • Password expired
  • Schema name not included in connection string

How to Fix

  1. Verify credentials with SQL*Plus:
sqlplus username/password@mydb
  1. Check if account is locked:
SELECT account_status, lock_date, expiry_date
FROM dba_users
WHERE username = 'MYUSER';
  1. Unlock the account:
ALTER USER myuser ACCOUNT UNLOCK;
  1. Reset the password:
ALTER USER myuser IDENTIFIED BY newpassword;
  1. Check password profile settings:
SELECT * FROM dba_profiles WHERE profile = 'DEFAULT';
  1. Verify connection string format:
# Correct format
sqlplus myuser/mypassword@//dbhost:1521/mydb

# With schema
sqlplus myuser/mypassword@mydb

Examples

$ sqlplus testuser/testpass@mydb
ERROR: ORA-01017: invalid username/password; logon denied

$ sqlplus testuser/CorrectPass@mydb
Connected.

-- Check failed login attempts
SELECT username, failed_login_attempts, lock_date
FROM dba_users
WHERE username = 'TESTUSER';

Related Errors

  • Permission error
  • Connection error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-01031: insufficient privileges
item.description:
ORA-01031: insufficient privileges occurs when a database user attempts an operation they do not have permission for. This commonly affects DDL operations, PL/SQL compilation, and data access.

Common Causes

  • User lacks CREATE TABLE, CREATE VIEW, or other DDL privileges
  • Missing SELECT on system tables or views
  • PL/SQL procedure requires grants the user does not have
  • Role not granted or not activated
  • SYSDBA/SYSOPER privilege required but not granted

How to Fix

  1. Check current user’s privileges:
SELECT * FROM session_privs;
SELECT * FROM session_roles;
  1. Grant required privileges:
-- Connect as DBA
GRANT CREATE TABLE TO myuser;
GRANT CREATE VIEW TO myuser;
GRANT CREATE PROCEDURE TO myuser;
GRANT CREATE SEQUENCE TO myuser;
  1. Grant system privileges via role:
CREATE ROLE app_developer;
GRANT CREATE TABLE, CREATE VIEW, CREATE PROCEDURE TO app_developer;
GRANT app_developer TO myuser;
  1. Grant specific object privileges:
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON schema.table_name TO myuser;
GRANT SELECT ON dba_tab_columns TO myuser;
  1. For SYSDBA operations:
-- Connect as SYSDBA
sqlplus / as sysdba
GRANT SYSDBA TO myuser;
  1. Check granted roles:
SELECT grantee, granted_role, admin_option
FROM dba_role_privs
WHERE grantee = 'MYUSER';

Examples

-- Error: ORA-01031: insufficient privileges
CREATE TABLE test (id NUMBER);
-- ORA-01031: insufficient privileges

-- Fix: grant privilege
-- As DBA:
GRANT CREATE TABLE TO myuser;

-- Then retry
CREATE TABLE test (id NUMBER);
-- Table created.

Related Errors

  • Auth error
  • Lock error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-01555: snapshot too old
item.description:
ORA-01555: snapshot too old (rollback segment too small) occurs when Oracle cannot maintain a read-consistent view of data because the undo information needed by a long-running query has been overwritten by newer transactions.

Common Causes

  • Long-running query spanning many DML operations
  • UNDO tablespace too small to retain enough history
  • FETCH across COMMIT in cursor operations
  • Batch processing without periodic commits
  • UNDO_RETENTION too short for the workload

How to Fix

  1. Check UNDO tablespace usage:
SELECT tablespace_name, round(sum_bytes/1024/1024/1024, 2) as size_gb
FROM (
 SELECT tablespace_name, sum(bytes) as sum_bytes
 FROM dba_data_files
 WHERE tablespace_name = (SELECT value FROM v$parameter WHERE name = 'undo_tablespace')
 GROUP BY tablespace_name
);
  1. Increase UNDO tablespace size:
ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/mydb/undotbs01.dbf'
AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 1G MAXSIZE 30G;
  1. Increase UNDO retention:
ALTER SYSTEM SET UNDO_RETENTION = 3600; -- 1 hour
  1. Use FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for concurrent processing:
DECLARE
 CURSOR c_orders IS
 SELECT order_id FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending'
 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;
BEGIN
 FOR rec IN c_orders LOOP
 -- process without blocking
 UPDATE orders SET status = 'processing' WHERE order_id = rec.order_id;
 COMMIT;
 END LOOP;
END;
  1. Optimize long-running queries:
-- Add indexes to reduce query time
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

-- Use parallel query for large scans
SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(4) */ * FROM large_table;
  1. Increase PGA for sort operations:
ALTER SYSTEM SET pga_aggregate_target = 4G;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-01555: snapshot too old: rollback segment number 15 with name "_SYSSMU15$" too small
DECLARE
 CURSOR c IS SELECT * FROM large_table;
BEGIN
 FOR rec IN c LOOP
 DBMS_LOCK.SLEEP(1); -- simulate slow processing
 -- Other sessions modifying large_table
 END LOOP;
END;

-- Fix: commit periodically or increase UNDO
ALTER SYSTEM SET UNDO_RETENTION = 7200;

Related Errors

  • Lock error
  • Tablespace error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-01653: unable to extend table
item.description:
ORA-01653: unable to extend table occurs when Oracle cannot allocate additional space for a table because the tablespace has no more free extents. This typically happens when the tablespace is full or autoextend is not enabled.

Common Causes

  • Tablespace is full and autoextend is disabled
  • Datafile maxsize reached
  • Large INSERT or bulk load consuming remaining space
  • Tablespace not configured with autoextend
  • Temporary tablespace full

How to Fix

  1. Check tablespace usage:
SELECT tablespace_name, round(sum_bytes/1024/1024/1024, 2) as used_gb,
 round(max_bytes/1024/1024/1024, 2) as max_gb,
 round((sum_bytes/max_bytes)*100, 1) as pct_used
FROM (
 SELECT tablespace_name,
 sum(bytes) as sum_bytes,
 sum(maxbytes) as max_bytes
 FROM dba_data_files
 GROUP BY tablespace_name
);
  1. Add datafile to tablespace:
ALTER TABLESPACE users
ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/mydb/users02.dbf'
SIZE 10G
AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 1G MAXSIZE 50G;
  1. Enable autoextend on existing datafile:
ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/mydb/users01.dbf'
AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 1G MAXSIZE 50G;
  1. Resize existing datafile:
ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/mydb/users01.dbf'
RESIZE 20G;
  1. Drop unused objects to free space:
SELECT segment_name, segment_type, bytes/1024/1024 as size_mb
FROM dba_segments
WHERE tablespace_name = 'USERS'
ORDER BY bytes DESC;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-01653: unable to extend table EMPLOYEES by 128 in tablespace USERS
INSERT INTO employees SELECT * FROM large_source_table;
-- ORA-01653: unable to extend table EMPLOYEES by 128 in tablespace USERS

-- Fix: add datafile
ALTER TABLESPACE users
ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/mydb/users03.dbf'
SIZE 5G AUTOEXTEND ON;

Related Errors

  • Sequence error
  • Tablespace error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-04091: table is mutating
item.description:
ORA-04091: table is mutating occurs when a row-level trigger tries to read or modify the table that fired it. Oracle prevents this to avoid inconsistent reads during the trigger’s execution.

Common Causes

  • Row-level trigger queries the same table
  • Trigger tries to INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE the firing table
  • Constraint validation queries the same table
  • Compound triggers not used for cross-row operations
  • Trying to enforce a running total or aggregate

How to Fix

  1. Use compound triggers (Oracle 11g+):
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER emp_salary_check
FOR INSERT OR UPDATE ON employees
COMPOUND TRIGGER
 TYPE salary_rec IS RECORD (
 emp_id NUMBER,
 new_salary NUMBER
 );
 TYPE salary_list IS TABLE OF salary_rec;
 pending_changes salary_list := salary_list();

 AFTER EACH ROW IS
 BEGIN
 pending_changes.EXTEND;
 pending_changes(pending_changes.LAST).emp_id := :NEW.employee_id;
 pending_changes(pending_changes.LAST).new_salary := :NEW.salary;
 END AFTER EACH ROW;

 AFTER STATEMENT IS
 BEGIN
 FOR i IN 1 .. pending_changes.COUNT LOOP
 -- Safe to query table here (statement-level)
 NULL;
 END LOOP;
 pending_changes.DELETE;
 END AFTER STATEMENT;
END emp_salary_check;
  1. Use a temporary table or PL/SQL collection to store values:
CREATE GLOBAL TEMPORARY TABLE temp_salary_check (
 emp_id NUMBER,
 new_salary NUMBER
) ON COMMIT DELETE ROWS;

CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER check_salary
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON employees
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
 INSERT INTO temp_salary_check VALUES (:NEW.employee_id, :NEW.salary);
END;
  1. Use autonomous transaction for isolated operations:
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE log_salary_change(
 p_emp_id NUMBER, p_new_salary NUMBER
) AS
 PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION;
BEGIN
 INSERT INTO salary_audit (emp_id, salary, changed_at)
 VALUES (p_emp_id, p_new_salary, SYSDATE);
 COMMIT;
END;
  1. Move validation to application level instead of trigger.

Examples

-- Error: ORA-04091: table EMPLOYEES is mutating
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER check_emp_count
AFTER INSERT ON employees
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
 IF (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees) > 100 THEN
 RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Too many employees');
 END IF;
END;

-- Fix: use compound trigger or statement-level check
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER check_emp_count
FOR INSERT ON employees
COMPOUND TRIGGER
 AFTER STATEMENT IS
 BEGIN
 IF (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees) > 100 THEN
 RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Too many employees');
 END IF;
 END AFTER STATEMENT;
END;

Related Errors

  • Lock error
  • Sequence error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-08004: sequence exceeds MAXVALUE
item.description:
ORA-08004: sequence exceeds MAXVALUE occurs when a sequence has reached its defined maximum value and cannot generate a new number. This typically happens with sequences that have CYCLE disabled or when the MAXVALUE is too small for the workload.

Common Causes

  • Sequence MAXVALUE reached
  • NOCYCLE option set on the sequence
  • MAXVALUE set too low during creation
  • Large batch inserts consuming sequence values rapidly
  • Sequence not configured with sufficient range

How to Fix

  1. Check current sequence value:
SELECT sequence_name, last_number, max_value, increment_by
FROM user_sequences
WHERE sequence_name = 'MY_SEQ';
  1. Alter the sequence to increase MAXVALUE:
ALTER SEQUENCE my_seq MAXVALUE 999999999;
  1. Reset the sequence value:
ALTER SEQUENCE my_seq INCREMENT BY -1000;
SELECT my_seq.NEXTVAL FROM dual; -- adjust current value
ALTER SEQUENCE my_seq INCREMENT BY 1;
  1. Recreate sequence with higher MAXVALUE:
DROP SEQUENCE my_seq;
CREATE SEQUENCE my_seq
 START WITH 1
 INCREMENT BY 1
 MAXVALUE 9999999999
 NOCYCLE
 CACHE 20;
  1. Use CYCLE option for sequences that should wrap around:
ALTER SEQUENCE my_seq CYCLE MAXVALUE 999999;
  1. Use identity columns (Oracle 12c+):
CREATE TABLE users (
 id NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY (START WITH 1 INCREMENT BY 1),
 name VARCHAR2(100)
);

Examples

-- Error: ORA-08004: sequence MY_SEQ exceeds MAXVALUE
SELECT my_seq.NEXTVAL FROM dual;
-- ORA-08004: sequence MY_SEQ exceeds MAXVALUE and cannot be instantiated

-- Fix: increase MAXVALUE
ALTER SEQUENCE my_seq MAXVALUE 99999999;
SELECT my_seq.NEXTVAL FROM dual;
-- 10001

Related Errors

  • Trigger error
  • Tablespace error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-12541: TNS no listener
item.description:
ORA-12541: TNS no listener occurs when the Oracle client cannot connect to the database because the TNS listener process is not running on the server or is not accessible on the configured port.

Common Causes

  • TNS listener service stopped or crashed
  • Listener listening on wrong port or interface
  • Firewall blocking port 1521
  • Incorrect tnsnames.ora entry
  • Oracle home not properly configured

How to Fix

  1. Check listener status:
lsnrctl status
  1. Start the listener:
lsnrctl start
  1. Verify listener configuration:
cat $ORACLE_HOME/network/admin/listener.ora
  1. Test connectivity with tnsping:
tnsping your_database_host:1521
  1. Fix tnsnames.ora entry:
# tnsnames.ora
MYDB =
 (DESCRIPTION =
 (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCP)(HOST = dbhost.example.com)(PORT = 1521))
 (CONNECT_DATA =
 (SERVICE_NAME = mydb)
 )
 )
  1. Check firewall:
telnet dbhost.example.com 1521
sudo ufw allow 1521/tcp

Examples

$ sqlplus user/password@mydb
ERROR: ORA-12541: TNS:no listener

$ lsnrctl status
LSNRCTL for Linux: Version 19.0.0.0.0
Connecting to (ADDRESS=(PROTOCOL=TCP)(HOST=0.0.0.0)(PORT=1521))
TNS-12541: TNS:no listener

Related Errors

  • Auth error
  • Permission error

item.title: Oracle - ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map
item.description:
ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition occurs when an INSERT statement tries to add a row with a partition key value that does not fall into any defined partition. This is common with range-partitioned tables when new date ranges are needed.

Common Causes

  • Partition range does not cover the inserted date/value
  • Missing partition for the current time period
  • MAXVALUE partition not defined
  • Monthly/weekly partitions not created for future dates
  • Incorrect partition key value

How to Fix

  1. Check existing partitions:
SELECT partition_name, high_value
FROM user_tab_partitions
WHERE table_name = 'SALES'
ORDER BY partition_position;
  1. Add a new partition for the missing range:
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_2024_01
VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-02-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'));
  1. Add a catch-all MAXVALUE partition:
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_future VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE);
  1. Use interval partitioning for automatic partition creation:
CREATE TABLE sales (
 sale_id NUMBER,
 sale_date DATE,
 amount NUMBER
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (sale_date)
INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1, 'MONTH'))
(
 PARTITION p_initial VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-01-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'))
);
  1. Split an existing partition to accommodate new values:
ALTER TABLE sales SPLIT PARTITION p_future
AT (TO_DATE('2024-06-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'))
INTO (PARTITION p_2024_h1, PARTITION p_future);
  1. Use DBMS_SCHEDULER to create future partitions automatically:
BEGIN
 DBMS_SCHEDULER.CREATE_JOB(
 job_name => 'CREATE_MONTHLY_PARTITIONS',
 job_type => 'PLSQL_BLOCK',
 job_action => 'BEGIN create_next_partition(); END;',
 start_date => SYSTIMESTAMP,
 repeat_interval => 'FREQ=MONTHLY;BYMONTHDAY=28',
 enabled => TRUE
 );
END;

Examples

-- Error: ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition
INSERT INTO sales VALUES (1, TO_DATE('2024-03-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 1000);
-- ORA-14400: inserted partition key does not map to any partition

-- Fix: add partition for March 2024
ALTER TABLE sales ADD PARTITION p_2024_03
VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2024-04-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'));

INSERT INTO sales VALUES (1, TO_DATE('2024-03-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 1000);
-- 1 row created.

Related Errors

  • Tablespace error
  • Lock error

item.title: Oracle Authentication Error
item.description:
Oracle Authentication Error

An Oracle authentication error occurs when the client cannot authenticate with the database server. The credentials are incorrect or the account is locked.

Common Causes

  • Incorrect username or password
  • Account locked due to failed attempts
  • Password expired
  • External authentication not configured

How to Fix

Verify Credentials

sqlplus username/password@mydb

Check Account Status

SELECT account_status FROM dba_users WHERE username = 'MYUSER';

Unlock Account

ALTER USER myuser ACCOUNT UNLOCK;

Reset Password

ALTER USER myuser IDENTIFIED BY new_password;

Check Password Expiry

SELECT username, expiry_date FROM dba_users WHERE username = 'MYUSER';

Fix External Authentication

# Check OS authentication
sqlplus / as sysdba

Examples

sqlplus user/password@mydb
ERROR: ORA-01017: invalid username/password; logon denied

# Check account status
SELECT account_status FROM dba_users WHERE username = 'USER';
-- LOCKED

Related Errors

  • Connection Error — connection failure
  • Permission Error — permission denied

item.title: Oracle Connection Error
item.description:
Oracle Connection Error

An Oracle connection error occurs when the Oracle client cannot connect to the database server. This is typically caused by TNS configuration issues or network problems.

Common Causes

  • TNS listener not running on server
  • Incorrect TNS entry in tnsnames.ora
  • Network connectivity issues
  • Firewall blocking listener port

How to Fix

Check TNS Listener

lsnrctl status

Verify TNS Configuration

cat $ORACLE_HOME/network/admin/tnsnames.ora

Test Connectivity

tnsping your_database_host:1521

Fix TNS Entry

# tnsnames.ora
MYDB =
 (DESCRIPTION =
 (ADDRESS = (PROTOCOL = TCP)(HOST = dbhost.example.com)(PORT = 1521))
 (CONNECT_DATA =
 (SERVICE_NAME = mydb)
 )
 )

Test Connection with SQLPlus

sqlplus user/password@mydb

Check Firewall

telnet dbhost.example.com 1521

Examples

sqlplus user/password@mydb
ERROR: ORA-12541: TNS:no listener

Related Errors

  • Auth Error — authentication failure
  • Permission Error — permission denied

item.title: Oracle LOB Error
item.description:
Oracle LOB Error

An Oracle LOB error occurs when operations on Large Object (LOB) data types (CLOB, BLOB, NCLOB, BFILE) fail. LOBs store large text or binary data.

Common Causes

  • LOB segment is full
  • LOB column exceeds maximum size
  • LOB locator is invalid or expired
  • LOB storage parameters not configured

How to Fix

Check LOB Segment Usage

SELECT table_name, column_name, segment_name
FROM user_lobs
WHERE table_name = 'MY_TABLE';

Check LOB Space

SELECT tablespace_name, SUM(bytes)/1024/1024 AS mb
FROM user_segments
WHERE segment_type = 'LOBSEGMENT'
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Add LOB Space

ALTER TABLE my_table MODIFY lob(my_clob) (
 ALTER STORAGE (NEXT 10M)
);

Read LOB Data

SELECT DBMS_LOB.SUBSTR(my_clob, 100, 1) FROM my_table WHERE id = 1;

Write LOB Data

UPDATE my_table SET my_clob = TO_CLOB('New content') WHERE id = 1;

Check LOB Inode

SELECT id, INSTR(my_clob, 'search text') FROM my_table;

Fix LOB Corruption

-- Check for corrupted LOBs
SELECT id FROM my_table
WHERE DBMS_LOB.INSTR(my_clob, CHR(0)) > 0;

Examples

INSERT INTO documents (id, content) VALUES (1, TO_CLOB('Large text...'));
-- ORA-01691: unable to extend lob segment SYS_LOB001

-- Fix: add tablespace
ALTER TABLESPACE lob_data ADD DATAFILE 'lob02.dbf' SIZE 1G;

Related Errors

  • Tablespace Error — tablespace issues
  • Permission Error — permission denied

item.title: Oracle Partition Error
item.description:
Oracle Partition Error

An Oracle partition error occurs when partition operations fail during creation, maintenance, or querying. Partitioning divides large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces.

Common Causes

  • Partition key not included in queries
  • Partition does not exist for the data range
  • Partition maintenance operations fail
  • Global index corruption after partition operations

How to Fix

Check Partition Information

SELECT partition_name, high_value
FROM user_tab_partitions
WHERE table_name = 'BIG_TABLE';

Add Partition

ALTER TABLE big_table ADD PARTITION p_2024
VALUES LESS THAN (TO_DATE('2025-01-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'));

Split Partition

ALTER TABLE big_table SPLIT PARTITION p_2024
AT (TO_DATE('2024-07-01', 'YYYY-MM-DD'))
INTO (PARTITION p_2024_h1, PARTITION p_2024_h2);

Drop Partition

ALTER TABLE big_table DROP PARTITION p_old;

Exchange Partition

ALTER TABLE big_table EXCHANGE PARTITION p_2024
WITH TABLE staging_table;

Query Partition

SELECT * FROM big_table PARTITION (p_2024);

Check Global Indexes

ALTER TABLE big_table MODIFY PARTITION p_2024
UPDATE GLOBAL INDEXES;

Examples

SELECT * FROM big_table WHERE date_col = '2024-01-01';
-- This works if date_col is the partition key

SELECT * FROM big_table WHERE other_col = 'value';
-- May scan all partitions (full table scan)

Related Errors

  • Tablespace Error — tablespace issues
  • LOB Error — LOB issues

item.title: Oracle Permission Denied Error
item.description:
Oracle Permission Denied Error

An Oracle permission denied error occurs when a user lacks the necessary database privileges to perform an operation. Oracle has a granular privilege system.

Common Causes

  • Missing SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE privileges
  • Object does not exist or is not accessible
  • Tablespace quota exceeded
  • System privilege not granted

How to Fix

Check User Privileges

SELECT * FROM user_role_privs;
SELECT * FROM user_sys_privs;

Grant Object Privileges

GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON mydb.users TO myuser;

Grant System Privileges

GRANT CREATE SESSION TO myuser;
GRANT CREATE TABLE TO myuser;

Check Tablespace Quota

SELECT tablespace_name, max_bytes
FROM dba_ts_quotas
WHERE username = 'MYUSER';

Grant Tablespace Quota

ALTER USER myuser QUOTA UNLIMITED ON users;

Check Object Exists

SELECT object_name, object_type FROM all_objects WHERE object_name = 'USERS';

Examples

SELECT * FROM admin.secrets;
ORA-00942: table or view does not exist

-- Fix: grant permission
GRANT SELECT ON admin.secrets TO myuser;

Related Errors

  • Auth Error — authentication failure
  • Tablespace Error — tablespace issues

item.title: Oracle Sequence Error
item.description:
Oracle Sequence Error

An Oracle sequence error occurs when a sequence fails to generate the next value. Sequences are used for auto-incrementing primary keys in Oracle.

Common Causes

  • Sequence exhausted (reached MAXVALUE)
  • Sequence cache issue
  • Sequence does not exist
  • ORDER/NOCORDER mismatch in RAC

How to Fix

Check Sequence Status

SELECT sequence_name, last_number, increment_by, max_value
FROM user_sequences;

Get Next Value

SELECT myseq.NEXTVAL FROM dual;

Reset Sequence

ALTER SEQUENCE myseq INCREMENT BY -100;
SELECT myseq.NEXTVAL FROM dual; -- Go back
ALTER SEQUENCE myseq INCREMENT BY 1;

Create New Sequence

CREATE SEQUENCE myseq
 START WITH 1
 INCREMENT BY 1
 NOCACHE
 NOCYCLE;

Fix Cache Issues

ALTER SEQUENCE myseq CACHE 20;

Handle MAXVALUE

-- Check current value
SELECT myseq.CURRVAL FROM dual;

-- Reset if exhausted
ALTER SEQUENCE myseq RESTART START WITH 1;

Examples

INSERT INTO users (id, name) VALUES (myseq.NEXTVAL, 'Alice');
ORA-08004: sequence MYSEQ.NEXTVAL exceeds MAXVALUE

-- Fix: alter sequence
ALTER SEQUENCE myseq MAXVALUE 999999999;

Related Errors

  • Constraint Error — unique constraint violation
  • Tablespace Error — tablespace issues

item.title: Oracle Synonym Error
item.description:
Oracle Synonym Error

An Oracle synonym error occurs when a synonym fails to resolve to its base object. Synonyms are aliases for database objects.

Common Causes

  • Base object does not exist or has been dropped
  • Synonym points to wrong schema
  • Synonym is not public and user lacks access
  • Object name changed after synonym creation

How to Fix

Check Synonym Status

SELECT synonym_name, table_owner, table_name
FROM user_synonyms;

Check Public Synonyms

SELECT synonym_name, table_owner, table_name
FROM dba_synonyms
WHERE owner = 'PUBLIC';

Create Synonym

-- Private synonym
CREATE SYNONYM mytable FOR schema.table_name;

-- Public synonym
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM mytable FOR schema.table_name;

Drop and Recreate Synonym

DROP SYNONYM mytable;
CREATE SYNONYM mytable FOR new_schema.new_table;

Check Object Exists

SELECT object_name, object_type, owner
FROM all_objects
WHERE object_name = 'TABLE_NAME';

Examples

CREATE SYNONYM emp FOR hr.employees;
SELECT * FROM emp;
-- Works if hr.employees exists

DROP TABLE hr.employees;
SELECT * FROM emp;
-- ORA-00942: table or view does not exist

Related Errors

  • View Error — view issues
  • Permission Error — permission denied

item.title: Oracle Tablespace Error
item.description:
Oracle Tablespace Error

An Oracle tablespace error occurs when the tablespace is full, has insufficient space, or encounters storage allocation issues. This prevents DDL and DML operations.

Common Causes

  • Tablespace is full (datafile cannot extend)
  • Tablespace autoextend is disabled
  • User quota on tablespace exceeded
  • Temporary tablespace is full

How to Fix

Check Tablespace Usage

SELECT tablespace_name,
 ROUND(SUM(bytes)/1024/1024) AS used_mb,
 ROUND(SUM(maxbytes)/1024/1024) AS max_mb
FROM dba_data_files
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Add Datafile

ALTER TABLESPACE users ADD DATAFILE '/path/to/newfile.dbf' SIZE 1G AUTOEXTEND ON;

Enable Autoextend

ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/path/to/file.dbf' AUTOEXTEND ON MAXSIZE 10G;

Check User Quota

SELECT tablespace_name, bytes, max_bytes
FROM dba_ts_quotas
WHERE username = 'MYUSER';

Increase User Quota

ALTER USER myuser QUOTA 5G ON users;

Check Temporary Tablespace

SELECT tablespace_name, SUM(bytes)/1024/1024 AS temp_mb
FROM dba_temp_files
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Examples

INSERT INTO big_table VALUES (1, 'data');
ORA-01653: unable to extend table ADMIN.BIG_TABLE by 128 in tablespace USERS

-- Fix: add datafile
ALTER TABLESPACE users ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/users02.dbf' SIZE 2G;

Related Errors

  • Permission Error — permission denied
  • Sequence Error — sequence issues

item.title: Oracle Trigger Error
item.description:
Oracle Trigger Error

An Oracle trigger error occurs when a database trigger fails during execution. Triggers are stored procedures that automatically execute in response to DML events.

Common Causes

  • Trigger body has PL/SQL errors
  • Mutating table error (ORA-04091)
  • Trigger references non-existent table
  • Recursion depth exceeded

How to Fix

Check Trigger Status

SELECT trigger_name, status FROM user_triggers;

Compile Trigger

ALTER TRIGGER mytrigger COMPILE;

Fix Mutating Table Error

-- Instead of querying the same table in a row trigger,
-- use a compound trigger (11g+)
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER my_trigger
FOR INSERT ON my_table
COMPOUND TRIGGER
 TYPE t_count IS TABLE OF NUMBER INDEX BY PLS_INTEGER;
 v_count t_count;
AFTER EACH ROW IS
BEGIN
 v_count(:NEW.id) := 1;
END AFTER EACH ROW;
AFTER STATEMENT IS
BEGIN
 NULL;
END AFTER STATEMENT;
END my_trigger;

Check for Compilation Errors

SHOW ERRORS TRIGGER mytrigger;

Disable/Enable Trigger

ALTER TRIGGER mytrigger DISABLE;
ALTER TRIGGER mytrigger ENABLE;

Check Trigger Event

SELECT trigger_event, triggering_event
FROM user_triggers
WHERE trigger_name = 'MYTRIGGER';

Examples

CREATE TRIGGER audit_trigger
BEFORE INSERT ON users
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
 :NEW.created_at := SYSDATE;
END;
/
-- ORA-04084: cannot change NEW values for this trigger type

Related Errors

  • View Error — view issues
  • Synonym Error — synonym issues

item.title: Oracle View Error
item.description:
Oracle View Error

An Oracle view error occurs when a view fails to compile or returns errors during querying. Views are stored SELECT statements that act as virtual tables.

Common Causes

  • Base table does not exist or has been dropped
  • Column references in view are invalid
  • Insufficient privileges on base tables
  • View contains syntax errors

How to Fix

Check View Status

SELECT object_name, status FROM user_objects WHERE object_type = 'VIEW';

Compile View

ALTER VIEW myview COMPILE;

Check View Definition

SELECT text FROM user_views WHERE view_name = 'MYVIEW';

Check Compilation Errors

SHOW ERRORS VIEW myview;

Recreate View

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW myview AS
SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE active = 1;

Check Dependencies

SELECT referenced_name, referenced_type
FROM user_dependencies
WHERE name = 'MYVIEW';

Grant View Permissions

GRANT SELECT ON myview TO another_user;

Examples

CREATE VIEW active_users AS
SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = 1;
-- View created

DROP TABLE users;
-- View becomes INVALID

ALTER VIEW active_users COMPILE;
-- ORA-00942: table or view does not exist

Related Errors

  • Trigger Error — trigger issues
  • Synonym Error — synonym issues
